cards.front: Compare autonomous AP and controller-based (lightweight) AP deployment models.
cards.back: Autonomous: each AP is a complete device, configured individually (CLI/web). Doesn't scale — every AP its own SSID, security, etc. Controller-based: APs are 'lightweight' — minimal local config, all policy/intelligence on a central WLC. Scales to hundreds of APs with consistent config and seamless roaming.

cards.front: What is CAPWAP, and what 2 separate tunnels does it use?
cards.back: Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points — the protocol between Lightweight APs and the WLC. Uses 2 UDP tunnels: Control (UDP 5246, encrypted DTLS — config, RRM, statistics) and Data (UDP 5247, optionally encrypted — wraps 802.11 frames).

Split-MAC (lightweight + WLC, default Cisco): real-time 802.11 functions (acks, retransmissions, beacons) handled by AP; non-real-time (auth, association, encryption keying, mgmt) handled by WLC. Local-MAC: AP handles everything, WLC just sets policy. Split-MAC enables centralized control.

cards.front: What is FlexConnect (formerly H-REAP), and when is it useful?
cards.back: Lightweight AP mode where the AP can switch user data LOCALLY (instead of tunneling to the WLC) when WAN to the WLC is slow. Useful in branch offices: APs report to a centralized WLC over WAN, but data stays on local LAN. Maintains operation if WAN to WLC fails.

cards.front: What's the difference between embedded WLC, cloud-based, and on-prem WLC?
cards.back: Embedded WLC: WLC software on the switch (like Cisco Catalyst 9800). On-prem hardware WLC: dedicated appliance (e.g., 9800-CL VM or 5520 hardware). Cloud-based: Cisco Meraki MR APs managed from cloud dashboard — no on-site WLC needed. Cloud is simplest; embedded saves rack space; on-prem is most powerful.

cards.front: What's an SSID-to-VLAN mapping, and why does it matter?
cards.back: Each SSID is mapped to a single VLAN at the WLC. Wireless clients on that SSID get DHCP from that VLAN's pool, and traffic is tagged onto that VLAN at the AP-to-switch trunk. Used to separate guest, employee, IoT into different VLANs/subnets/security policies.
